In the case of the residential sector, the range of variation of the implied emission factors is the smallest for  $\\mathrm{SO}_2$  and  $\\mathrm{NO}_x$ , but the largest for  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  and BC. For the transport sector a relatively large variation is present for CO, with an implied emission factor for China that is above the median. For agriculture it is remarkable that China and India, as well as the USA and EU 27, have implied emission factors that are above the median, with China reaching the maximum compared to all other world countries.", "Even though only implied emissions factors for country emissions are presented in Fig. 4, the implied emission factors were also calculated for the international bunker fuel and indicated that the implied emission factors are at the high end of the range for  $\\mathrm{SO}_2$  (0.98 ton  $\\mathrm{SO}_2\\mathrm{TJ}^{-1}$  similar to the road transport emission factor of Laos or Panama),  $\\mathrm{NO}_x$  (with 1.65 ton  $\\mathrm{NO}_x\\mathrm{TJ}^{-1}$  similar as for transport in Bangladesh or Myanmar),  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  (with 0.17 ton  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}\\mathrm{TJ}^{-1}$  similar as for transport in China), but are relatively low for CO, NMVOC and BC. The high  $\\mathrm{SO}_2$  implied emission factor (from EDGARv4.3) represents the use of lower quality fuels in sea transportation, especially in international waters:  $85\\%$  of the sea bunker fuel in 2010 consists of residual fuel oil with an emission factor of 1.29 ton  $\\mathrm{SO}_2\\mathrm{TJ}^{-1}$ ."], "chunk_type": "figure"}

evaluation.extracted_insight: Figure 4 presents box‑plot distributions of implied emission factors (ton TJ⁻¹) for 2010 across sectors, revealing that India has considerably higher factors for CO, PM₂.₅, BC and OC, while EU27 and the USA show the lowest values, indicating strong regional differences in emission intensity.
evaluation.reason: Delivers quantitative, region‑specific emission‑intensity data with detailed sector breakdowns, offering a solid basis for comparative analyses and model calibration.

orchestrator_plan.reason: 当前检索未涉及机器学习模型解释的可视化（如 SHAP、LIME、特征重要性热图），这是将预测结果透明化并辅助决策的关键新方向，相关文献价值高且创新度大。

content.text: Compared with traditional techniques for forecasting air pollution, namely empirical approaches and simple statistical models, deep learning approaches can handle complex and non-linear relationships among air quality variables which allow them to better capture the pollution episode forecasts. Therefore, deep learning has been identified as a vital tool for air quality modelling and forecasting, which helps in recognizing pollution hot spots (Schmidhuber, 2015; Ong et al., 2016; Li et al., 2017).

Due to the shutdown of industrial activities and traffic volume, an obvious decline in primary air pollutants was observed in most Chinese cities in 2020 (Liu et al., 2020; Yuan et al., 2021). This will undoubtedly cause high uncertainty while investigating the effect of CCTPs on air quality. Therefore, the following data is only extracted from 2013 to 2019, and the AQI in 2020 is predicted through a deep learning network. Subsequently, the investigated data covers 8 years before and after the policy node, namely from 2013 to 2020, which is adequate for policy evaluation (Yu et al., 2021). In the following, air quality and meteorological data are obtained via a web crawler.

content.text: Air quality models need to take differences in how volatile organic compounds (VOCs) react in the atmosphere when predicting effects of their emissions on secondary pollutants such as ozone or secondary organic aerosol. The chemical mechanism is the component of the models that represents how emitted compounds react and form secondary pollutants. Different mechanisms use different model species to represent how organic compounds react, with the number of species in different mechanisms varying by more than an order of magnitude. An important but often overlooked part of the air quality modeling process is determining which model species to use when representing the hundreds of organic compounds that are emitted.

content.text: Air quality prediction can be influenced by meteorological conditions, atmospheric dispersion, and geographic characteristics. Therefore, in addition to the air quality data, daily meteorological parameters such as the highest temperature (HT), lowest temperature (LT), wind direction (WD), and wind power (WP) are also considered. The identified parameters are taken as input along with the air quality parameters to encode the spatiotemporal relation, which is extracted from China's weather forecast and records platform (http://tianqi.2345.com/).

content.text: The figure displays grid maps for gaseous and aerosol species based on the MIX Asian emission inventory in 2010, highlighting spatial emission patterns. The maps illustrate improved spatial emission patterns due to the incorporation of high-resolution datasets, assisting in better understanding and management of emissions across Asia.

Compared to the previous gridded Asian emission inventories, we believe the spatial patterns are improved because several local high-resolution emission datasets are incorporated, such as CPED for China and JEI-DB and OPRF for Japan. However, for sectors in which emissions are dominated by spatially scattered sources (e.g., residential combustion, solvent use), the spatial distributions in emissions are still uncertain.", "Figure 12.", "Abstract. The MIX inventory is developed for the years 2008 and 2010 to support the Model Inter-Comparison Study for Asia (MICS-Asia) and the Task Force on Hemispheric Transport of Air Pollution (TF HTAP) by a mosaic of up-to-date regional emission inventories. Emissions are estimated for all major anthropogenic sources in 29 countries and regions in Asia. We conducted detailed comparisons of different regional emission inventories and incorporated the best available ones for each region into the mosaic inventory at a uniform spatial and temporal resolution. Emissions are aggregated to five anthropogenic sectors: power, industry, residential, transportation, and agriculture. We estimate the total Asian emissions of 10 species in 2010 as follows:  $51.3\mathrm{TgSO}_2$ ,  $52.1\mathrm{TgNO}_x$ ,  $336.6\mathrm{TgCO}$ ,  $67.0\mathrm{TgNMVOC}$  (non-methane volatile organic compounds),  $28.8\mathrm{TgNH}_3$", "Generally, there are two common approaches to develop a bottom-up emission inventory at regional level. One is using a unified framework of source categories, calculating method, chemical speciation scheme (if applicable), and spatial and temporal allocations (e.g., Streets et al., 2003; Ohara et al., 2007; Lu et al., 2011). Using the unified approach, emissions are estimated in a consistent way with attainable resources. Several Asian emission inventories widely used in the community were developed by the unified approach. Streets et al. (2003) first developed a comprehensive Asian emission inventory for a variety of gaseous and aerosol species for the year 2000 to support the TRACE-P (Transport and Chemical Evolution over the Pacific) campaign (Carmichael et al., 2003), which was subsequently used for MICS-Asia Phase II. Ohara et al. (2007) developed the Regional Emission inventory in Asia (REAS) version 1.1 covering emissions of major species over Asia from 1980 to 2003, which provides estimates of Asian emissions for a long-term period. However, with the unified approach, many region-dependent parameters are shared among different regions due to lack of resources and local knowledge (e.g., emission factors, chemical profiles, spatial proxies, and temporal profiles, etc.), introducing large uncertainties in emission estimates for a specific region (He et al., 2007; Kurokawa et al., 2009).", "The MIX inventory is developed for 2008 and 2010, in accordance with base year simulations in MICS-Asia III and the Task Force on Hemispheric Transport of Air Pollution (TF HTAP). It should be noted that MIX is not comparable to INTEX-B and TRACE-P to derive an emission trend due to differences in methodology and underlying data. In this paper, we also provided Asian emissions for 2006 using the same methodology, partly resolving the problems of trend analysis in mosaic inventories. The gridded MIX emission data for the years 2008 and 2010 are then incorporated", "Figure 1 presents the definition of the MIX domain and emission datasets used for each country and region. The domain of MIX covers 29 countries and regions (the full list of country and region names are listed in Table 1), stretching from Kazakhstan in the west to Russia Far East in the east and from Indonesia in the south to Siberia in the north. Emissions are aggregated into five sectors: power, industry, residential, transportation, and agriculture. Ten chemical species are included in the MIX inventory, including both gaseous and aerosol species:  $\mathrm{SO}_2$ ,  $\mathrm{NO}_x$ , CO, NMVOC (non-methane volatile organic compounds),  $\mathrm{NH}_3$  (ammonia),  $\mathrm{PM}_{10}$  (particulate matter with diameter less than or equal to  $10\mu \mathrm{m}$ ),  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  (particulate matter with diameter less than or equal to  $2.5\mu \mathrm{m}$ ), BC (black carbon), OC (organic carbon), and  $\mathrm{CO}_2$ . Only emissions from anthropogenic sources are included in MIX. Monthly emissions are provided by sector at  $0.25^{\circ} \times 0.25^{\circ}$  resolution. Gridded emissions are available from http://www.meicmodel.org/dataset-mix. Figure 4 presents the emission distributions among sectors over Asia in 2010. Among the different sectors, the industrial sector has the largest contribution to  $\mathrm{SO}_2$  (50% of total), NMVOC (38%),  $\mathrm{PM}_{10}$  (48%), and  $\mathrm{CO}_2$  (40%) emissions. Emission estimates from bottom-up inventories are uncertain due to lack of complete knowledge of human activities and emission from different sources. Uncertainty ranges of an emission inventory could be estimated using propagation of error or Monte Carlo approaches (e.g., Streets et al., 2003; Zhao et al., 2011). content.text: The figure shows air pollutant concentrations (NO, NO2, CO, PM2.5) at the inlet and outlet of a Shanghai tunnel over time using line charts. The figure illustrates higher pollutant concentrations at the tunnel outlet, highlighting the impact of vehicle emissions within the tunnel.

The tunnel is called East Yan'an Road tunnel, is an underwater tunnel crossing the Huangpu River in Shanghai. It has two bores with two lanes for each direction. The total length is  $2,107\\mathrm{m}$ ; cross-sectional area is  $33.75\\mathrm{m}^2$ . To continuously monitor major air pollutants from vehicle exhaust, we installed two integrated air pollution monitoring units at  $150\\mathrm{m}$  away from the inlet and outlet of the tunnel, respectively. The height of the unit was about  $1.2\\mathrm{m}$ . Fig. 2 shows the location of the tunnel and the monitoring sites. There were 4 sensors which could separately monitor NO,  $\\mathrm{NO}_2$ , CO, and  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  concentrations in each unit. NO,  $\\mathrm{NO}_2$ , and CO were detected using electrochemical method. Their detection limits were  $0.01\\mathrm{mg}\\mathrm{m}^{-3}$ ,  $0.01\\mathrm{mg}\\mathrm{m}^{-3}$ , and  $0.15\\mathrm{mg}\\mathrm{m}^{-3}$ , respectively.  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  was detected by light scattering method. Its resolution was  $0.1\\mu \\mathrm{g}\\mathrm{m}^{-3}$ . The monitoring data was continuously transmitted to a remote database through wireless network. The system was built in July 2016 and has been operated by now. To ensure the reliability of monitoring", "Where,  $EF(\\mathrm{g}\\cdot \\mathrm{km}^{-1})$  is the average emission factor per vehicle per kilometer of fleet in the tunnel.  $C_{outlet}$  and  $C_{inlet}$ $(\\mathrm{mg}\\cdot \\mathrm{m}^{-3})$  are air pollutant concentrations at outlet and inlet of the tunnel.  $A(\\mathrm{m}^2)$  is the cross-sectional area of the tunnel  $(\\mathrm{A} = 33.75\\mathrm{m}^2)$ .  $\\nu (\\mathrm{m}\\cdot \\mathrm{s}^{-1})$  is air velocity parallel to the tunnel monitored by an operation management company of the tunnel. Simulation domain.", "save_path": "C:\liuxingyu\multisubspace-data\predataprocess\paper_md\Estimation of biogenic VOC emissions and their cor\figure_1.jpg", "functional_roles": {"main_role": "Data & Experimental Setup", "sub_roles": ["Introduce Experimental Setup"]}, "visual_type": {"main_type": "Geospatial Visualizations", "sub_types": ["Map"]}, "key_entities": ["MEGAN", "CMAQ", "WRF model", "BVOC emissions", "Tibetan Plateau", "Inner Mongolia", "PRD", "BTH", "SCB", "YRD"], "concise_summary": "The figure depicts the simulation domain over China, illustrating terrain height and key regions like Tibetan Plateau and Inner Mongolia.", "inferred_insight": "The figure underscores the simulation domain's geographic and topographic details crucial for understanding BVOC emissions and air quality modeling over China.", "references": ["MEGAN v2.1 (Guenther et al. 2012) was utilized to estimate BVOC emissions in China with a  $27\mathrm{-km}$  horizontal grid spacing domain (shown in Fig. 1). The inputs for MEGAN include the LAI, PFTs, emission factors, and meteorological data (e.g., solar radiation, temperature, relative humidity and soil moisture). The monthly average LAI was obtained from the 8-day MODIS LAI product (MOD15A2, 2017) with the same horizontal resolution as the study domain, as shown in Fig. 2. The PFT map was obtained from the MODIS MCD12Q1 product and regridded into the WRF domain to compute the fraction of each PFT in each grid (Fig. 3). The default MEGAN emission factors  $(\mathrm{mg~m}^{-2}\mathrm{h}^{-1})$  with a resolution of  $30\mathrm{s}$ $(< 1\mathrm{km})$ , which reflect the BVOC emission rate under standard canopy conditions, were leveraged (http://lar.wsu.edu/megan/guides.html). Meteorological conditions were simulated by the WRF model. A one-year MEGAN simulation was performed for 2017 in China.", "We set up Community Multiscale Air Quality model (CMAQ version 5.2.1, Foroutan et al. (2017)) (https://www.cmascenter.org/cmaq/) to simulate the atmospheric composition over China with the same domain and grid resolution for WRF and MEGAN. The initial and boundary conditions for CMAQ simulation were based on the CMAQ default profiles which represent unpolluted atmosphere. The CB05 represented the gas phase, and AERO6 represented the aerosol chemical mechanisms (Appel et al. 2013). For the simulation of SOA, we used the default SOA module of the CMAQv5.2.1. It simulates NMVOC-derived SOA with a two-product model, treats primary organic aerosol (POA) as nonvolatile and nonreactive, and ignores IVOC emissions. Carlton et al. (2010) provided a detailed description of aerosol chemistry in CMAQ, including SOA formation from benzene, isoprene and sesquiterpenes. The POA oxidation mechanism is described in Simon and Bhave (2012). The new updates in CMAQ v5.2.1 accounts for the semivolatile", "partitioning and gas-phase aging of these POA compounds consistent with experimentally derived parameterizations (Murphy et al. 2017), SOA properties and IEPOX organosulfate formation rate constant were updated (Pye et al. 2017). We selected July as representative of summertime and acquired anthropogenic emissions for the CMAQ domain from the MEIC emission inventory 2016 developed by Tsinghua University (http://www.meicmodel.org/), which contains monthly gridded  $(0.25\times 0.25^{\circ})$  emissions information for anthropogenic emissions in the CB05 mechanism. The biogenic emissions were modeled using MEGAN (described in Section 2.2).", "Fig. S1 and S2 show the seasonal spatial distribution of temperature at  $2\mathrm{m}$  (T2) and the daily downward shortwave radiation (DSW) simulated by the WRF model. And these meteorological factors were evaluated using in situ measurement data from 824 and 84 national meteorological sites, respectively. The in situ measurement data are from the China Meteorological Data Sharing Service System (http://data.cma.cn/). Table 2 presents the verification statistics for the average daily T2 and DSW among all sites. As shown in Table 2, the mean error (ME), mean bias (MB), correlation coefficient (r) and root mean square error (RMSE) of station-averaged hourly T2 series are 2.47, -1.26, 0.95 and  $2.59^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , respectively. The simulation yields cooling biases of  $-0.98$ ,  $-0.90$ ,  $-1.70$  and  $-1.18^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$  in spring, summer, fall and winter, respectively. The ME, MB, r and RMSE values for the annual DSW series are 36.00,  $-29.88$ , 0.80 and  $81.33\mathrm{Wm}^{-2}$ , and the DSW simulation yields underestimations of 32.12, 43.61, 24.25 and  $19.38\mathrm{Wm}^{-2}$  in spring, summer, fall and winter, respectively. It is because that the WRF model overestimate the cloud coverage which lead to the slightly underestimation on T2 and DSW (Wang et al. 2010; Wen et al. 2014). Compared with other studies on yearly long WRF simulation in China (Wang et al., 2014; Ying et al. 2014; Zhang et al., 2012), these biases are", "Fig. 2. Seasonal spatial distribution of the LAI in the model domain.", "between our results and those of Klinger et al. (2002). It is mainly because that Klinger et al. (2002) did not include emissions from the shrubs and assumed that BVOC emissions emitted from forests in China with  $251,283\mathrm{km}^2$  which is smaller than the domain of our study. Therefore, variations in these factors in different years may be the cause of the uncertainty in the modeling simulations. However, large discrepancies in the emission estimates still exist among this study and previous studies. These differences result from the use of different emission factors, land cover distributions, meteorological conditions and model algorithms in developing the emission inventories, as discussed above.", "Fig. 3. Seasonal distributions of the proportions of PFTs in the model domain.", "Fig. 7a-c shows the simulated and observed daily 1-h maximum  $\mathrm{O_3}$ $(\mathrm{DM1O_3})$  concentration over China in July 2017. In general, the model successfully captured the spatial pattern over the simulated domain with highest  $\mathrm{DM1O_3}$  areas centralized in the east, especially in the BTH, PRD, YRD and SCB regions due to the relatively large  $\mathrm{NO_x}$  and VOC emission and favorable meteorological conditions for ozone formation in these areas (e.g., little rain and strong solar radiation). source_args.query_intent: mixed‑reality holographic 3D visualization of urban pollutant plumes

content.text: The figure shows the geographical location of Shenzhen and the sampling site within the Pearl River Delta region using maps and satellite imagery. The figure emphasizes the strategic location of the sampling site in Shenzhen for monitoring atmospheric pollutants, representing typical conditions for the Pearl River Delta region.

{"paper_title": "Long-term observations of oxygenated volatile orga", "figure_id": "img_147", "figure_title": "Fig. 1. (a) Shenzhen is located on the southeast coast of China; (b) the sampling site of this study and its surrounding environment in the Pearl River Delta region (satellite images from Google Earth).", "save_path": "C:\liuxingyu\multisubspace-data\predataprocess\paper_md\Long-term observations of oxygenated volatile orga\figure_1.jpg", "functional_roles": {"main_role": "Data & Experimental Setup", "sub_roles": ["Introduce Experimental Setup", "Showcase Dataset Samples"]}, "visual_type": {"main_type": "Geospatial Visualizations", "sub_types": ["Map", "Satellite Image"]}, "key_entities": ["Shenzhen", "Pearl River Delta", "Peking University Shenzhen Graduate School", "PM2.5", "VOCs", "O3"], "concise_summary": "The figure shows the geographical location of Shenzhen and the sampling site within the Pearl River Delta region using maps and satellite imagery.", "inferred_insight": "The figure emphasizes the strategic location of the sampling site in Shenzhen for monitoring atmospheric pollutants, representing typical conditions for the Pearl River Delta region.", "references": ["As presented in Fig. 1, Shenzhen is located in the PRD of Guangdong Province, China. The sampling site for this study was located on the campus of the Shenzhen Graduate School of Peking University (PKUSZ,  $22.60^{\circ}\mathrm{N}$ ,  $113.97^{\circ}\mathrm{E}$ ) in western Shenzhen, which is predominantly surrounded by green areas, teaching buildings and apartments. No evident anthropogenic emission sources of air pollutants surrounded the sampling site, except a highway approximately  $100\mathrm{m}$  away. Previous studies have shown that the concentrations of air pollutants such as  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  and VOCs at this site were approximately equal to their average concentrations in the entire PRD region (Louie et al., 2013; Huang et al., 2018). Therefore, this site was considered suitable for long-term observation of trends in air pollutants in the PRD region. Table S1 summarizes the effective days of VOCs observed in each month from 2014 to 2019, with 1169 days in total over the six years of sampling. During the six-year sampling period, the loss of data was mainly due to instrument failure and bad weather, especially in the wet season due to frequent rainfall, resulting in a higher rate of missing data during", "Shenzhen is located in China's Pearl River Delta (PRD) urban agglomeration, a region where rapid urbanization and industrialization have led to serious, complex atmospheric pollution (Sin et al., 2001; He et al., 2011). With the implementation of strict environmental protection measures in recent years, the  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  concentration in Shenzhen has decreased significantly. From 2013 to 2019, the average value of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  in Shenzhen was reduced from 40 to  $24~\mu \mathrm{g} / \mathrm{m}^3$  (Ecological Environment Bureau of Shenzhen Municipality, 2019). However, due to the high temperatures and high solar radiation characteristics of this region, atmospheric photochemical reactions are active, and the concentration of  $\mathrm{O_3}$  was increasing at an average annual rate of 0.7 ppb from 2014 to 2018 (Wang et al., 2020), such that  $\mathrm{O_3}$  is replacing  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  as the primary atmospheric pollutant in the region (Huang et al., 2019). Several previous short-term studies have observed the diurnal and seasonal patterns of OVOCs and identified their sources in this region (Liu et al., 2008; Liu et al., 2012; Louie et al., 2013; Han et al., 2019; Huang et al., 2019; Zhu et al., 2019); however, this is the first long-term study of OVOCs that permits the observation of interannual variability and long-term changes, in particular those related to emission reduction measures.", "j.envpol.2019.113651.

content.text: Vertical profiles show particle concentrations upwind and downwind of a motorway, revealing height-dependent differences and correlations with NOx levels. The figure illustrates how particle concentrations vary with height and time, confirming correlations with NOx and revealing impacts of emissions on air quality.

Upwind the motorway, no height-dependent difference in the particle number and surface area concentration was observed. On the downwind side, substantially higher concentrations were measured within the plume from near the ground up to  $20\\mathrm{m}$  height than above the plume (Fig. 5). This height dependency was found for particles with diameter  $< 300\\mathrm{nm}$ , which are soot particles and nucleation mode particles formed by condensation of exhaust gases in most cases and for coarse particles (diameter  $>1\\mu \\mathrm{m}$ ), originating from tyre and brake abrasion and resuspension of soil dust. Consequently, the results in this study confirmed the correlation between particles and  $\\mathrm{NO}_x$  values that had also been observed in former case studies. A linear relation between the particle surface area (SA) and  $\\mathrm{NO}_x$  with correlation coefficients of  $R^2 = 0.96$  and 0.89 for 2 downwind cases was found. At around  $30\\mathrm{m}$  above ground, the background value of SA was attained. By subtracting the background value from the concentrations measured in the near-surface air layer, direct emissions of the road were obtained, even if contemporaneous upwind and downwind measurements were missing. Together with the parallel wind measurements, mean absolute emission factors (EF) were determined. Source profiles of five-factor solution derived from the PMF modelling using the initial dataset. The box and whisker plots are constructed according to the 25-75th and 1-99th percentiles of the bootstrap results  N = 100 . The squares represent the median values of bootstrap. SU: solvent usage, CS: combustion sources, VE: vehicle emissions, GE: gasoline evaporation, IN: industrial sources.", "save_path": "C:\\liuxingyu\\multisubspace-data\\predataprocess\\paper_md\\Source apportionment of volatile organic compounds\\figure_4.jpg", "functional_roles": {"main_role": "Results & Findings", "sub_roles": ["Present Core Findings", "Present Quantitative Results"]}, "visual_type": {"main_type": "Quantitative Charts", "sub_types": ["Box Plot"]}, "key_entities": ["PMF modelling", "source profiles", "bootstrap results", "solvent usage", "combustion sources", "vehicle emissions", "gasoline evaporation", "industrial sources", "VOC"], "concise_summary": "The figure displays box plots of source profiles from PMF modelling derived from an initial dataset, highlighting median values and percentile ranges for five factors: solvent usage, combustion sources, vehicle emissions, gasoline evaporation, and industrial sources.", "inferred_insight": "The figure provides insight into the different source contributions to VOCs, with significant variation noted between emissions profiles and the influence of atmospheric photochemistry on VOC composition and source identification.", "references": ["Fig. 4 shows the source profiles derived from the PMF bootstrap results. The diurnal variations of each source are shown in Fig. 5. The Pearson correlations between VOC source contributions, meteorological parameters, and other air pollutants were analyzed to support the source identification results (Table S5).", "results by the PMF modelling. The measured and initial datasets were both introduced into the PMF model and the source profiles (Fig. 4 and Fig. S3) from two datasets were compared as shown in Fig. 8. The source profiles of gasoline evaporation, solvent usage, combustion sources, and industrial sources derived from two different datasets were in line with each other, with Pearson coefficient  $(r^2)$  of 0.99, 0.85, 0.68, and 0.40, respectively. However, a great discrepancy was found for vehicle emissions. The PMF algorithms aim to find the profiles and contributions for a given number of factors that have the minimum weighed residues of the input dataset. If the importance of atmospheric photochemical processes exceeds the differences of emission profiles from various sources, the factors in PMF can still be identified according to the different degrees of photochemical processing (Yuan et al., 2012). The source profiles derived from the two datasets suggested that the importance of photochemistry in the atmosphere exceeded the differences in the VOC compositions of vehicle emissions. Compared to the source apportionment from the measured dataset, the contributions of solvent usage, gasoline evaporation, and industrial sources to total VOC mixing ratio derived from the initial dataset were higher, while vehicle emissions and combustion sources were lower (Fig. S4). The differences suggested that the VOC decay should be considered before source apportionment using the receptor model."], "chunk_type": "figure"}

metadata.metadata:
{"paper_title": "Source apportionment of volatile organic compounds", "figure_id": "img_203", "figure_title": "Fig. 6. Percentages  \%  of different VOC sources to the total VOC mixing ratio (MR), OH loss rate  (L_{OH)} , ozone formation potential (OFP), and secondary organic aerosol potential (SOAP).", "save_path": "C:\liuxingyu\multisubspace-data\predataprocess\paper_md\Source apportionment of volatile organic compounds\figure_6.jpg", "functional_roles": {"main_role": "Results & Findings", "sub_roles": ["Present Quantitative Results"]}, "visual_type": {"main_type": "Quantitative Charts", "sub_types": ["Bar Chart"]}, "key_entities": ["VOCs", "OH loss rate", "L_{OH}", "ozone formation potential", "OFP", "secondary organic aerosol potential", "SOAP", "combustion sources", "vehicle emissions", "gasoline evaporation", "industrial sources", "solvent usage"], "concise_summary": "The figure displays the contribution percentages of various VOC sources to metrics such as MR, L_{OH}, OFP, and SOAP.", "inferred_insight": "The dominant sources differ across metrics; combustion sources lead in MR, while industrial sources dominate L_{OH} and SOAP, and vehicle emissions are highest for OFP.", "references": ["Fig. 6 shows the contributions of different VOC sources to the total VOC mixing ratio. The combustion sources contributed most (39.0% or  $23.9 \pm 13.6$  ppbv) to the total VOCs in winter, followed by vehicle emissions (22.5%,  $14.0 \pm 11.3$  ppbv), gasoline evaporation (14.2%,  $8.83 \pm 7.11$  ppbv), industrial sources (12.6%,  $7.92 \pm 10.2$  ppbv), and solvent usage (11.6%,  $6.98 \pm 5.65$  ppbv), respectively.", "As shown in Fig. 6, industrial sources contributed  $32.2\%$  to the total OH loss rate, followed by solvent usage  $(21.4\%)$ , combustion sources  $(20.4\%)$ , vehicle emissions  $(19.4\%)$ , and gasoline evaporation  $(6.61\%)$  respectively. Usually, vehicle emissions or petrochemical industrial emissions dominate man-made VOC reactivity in urban areas. For instance, motor vehicle emissions (dominated by high fractions of aromatics and alkenes) contributed to  $50\%$  of the calculated VOC reactivity in Pasadena (Borbon et al., 2013). Similarly,  $\mathrm{C}_2 \sim \mathrm{C}_6$  alkenes and aromatics from the petrochemical refinery and motor vehicles accounted for  $42\%$  of the total calculated VOC reactivity  $(7.5~\mathrm{s}^{-1})$  in the Houston/ Galveston Bay region (Gilman et al., 2009). The percentages of vehicle emissions and industrial sources to OH loss rate in this study were lower than those in the two above researches. However, in this study, the VOC reactivity  $(2.79~\mathrm{s}^{-1})$  contributed by vehicle emissions was higher than the traffic-related reactivity  $(1.2~\mathrm{s}^{-1})$  in the Northern Front Range Metropolitan Area of Colorado (Abeleira et al., 2017).", "For the OFP of different sources (Fig. 6), vehicle emissions had the largest contribution  $(27.8\%)$ , followed by industrial sources  $(25.4\%)$ , solvent usage  $(20.9\%)$ , combustion sources  $(18.9\%)$ , and gasoline evaporation  $(7.04\%)$ , respectively. The OFP of vehicle emissions in this study was comparable to a previous study in Wuhan that vehicle exhausts accounted for  $23.4 \pm 1.3\%$  of  $\mathrm{O}_3$  formation (Lyu et al., 2016). However, the contribution of industrial sources to OFP was lower than", "Volatile organic compounds (VOCs) are important contributors to atmospheric chemical reactivity rate  $(\mathrm{L_{OH}})$ , ozone formation potential (OFP), and secondary organic aerosol formation potential (SOAP). The purpose of this study was to determine the contributions of different VOC sources to  $\mathrm{L_{OH}}$ , OFP, and SOAP using positive matrix factorization (PMF) model. Hourly observation of ambient VOCs at a megacity of Central China was conducted for one month in winter. The  $\mathrm{L_{OH}}$ , OFP, and SOAP of VOC species were firstly estimated. The PMF model was then used to apportion their sources. Five VOC sources including solvent usage, gasoline evaporation, combustion sources, vehicle emissions, and industrial sources were apportioned with their average contributions of  $11.6\%$ ,  $14.2\%$ ,  $39.0\%$ ,  $22.5\%$ , and  $12.6\%$ , respectively. Industrial sources were the highest contributors to the  $\mathrm{L_{OH}}$  and SOAP with average contributions of  $32.2\%$  and  $32.8\%$ , respectively, while vehicle emissions contributed most to the OFP  $(27.8\%)$ . This study highlighted that the VOCs and ozone pollution control should give priority to the reactivity (OFP and SOAP) based strategies rather than concentration contribution-based strategies.", "Alkanes  
Fig. 3. Individual contributions  $(\%)$  of different VOC species (outer circular bar plots) and mean contributions  $(\%)$  (inner donut charts) of alkanes, alkenes, acetylene, and aromatics to the OH loss rate (a), ozone formation potential (b), and secondary organic aerosol potential (c).", "Fig. 7. Percentages  $(\%)$  of different VOC sources to the total VOC mixing ratio (MR), reactivity  $\mathrm{(L_{OH})}$ , ozone formation potential (OFP), and secondary organic aerosol potential (SOAP) under different  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  concentration ranges.", "To better understand the differences in levels and reactivity of VOC sources under different air quality, the  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  levels were classified into several groups and the source contributions, OH loss rate, OFP, and SOAP of VOCs were summarized in Fig. 7. Generally, with the increase of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  levels from clean to heavy pollution, the VOC levels also increased from  $40.8 \pm 17.6$  ppbv to  $89.6 \pm 26.3$  ppbv. The same phenomenon was also reported elsewhere (Han et al., 2017; Liu et al., 2017; Hui et al., 2019). Fig. 7 clearly shows that the high  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  pollution was characterized by an increase of industrial sources, which accounted for up to  $27.3\%$ ,  $56.2\%$ ,  $48.4\%$ , and  $57.0\%$  of VOCs mixing ratio, OH loss rate, OFP, and SOAP, respectively. The results here were similar to a previous study that the percentage of industrial sources increased significantly with the increase in VOC levels and this source dominated the VOC origins during an entire severe pollution episode (Hui et al.,", "2019). On the contrary, the contributions of combustion sources, vehicle emissions, and solvent usage to OH loss rate, OFP, and SOAP decreased with the increase of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  levels. Despite a decreasing trend of combustion sources to the VOC mixing ratio from clean to pollution, it still dominated the VOC origins during the heavy pollution period. Therefore, to alleviate the VOCs pollution, it should prior control combustion sources. From the perspective of VOC reactivity, OFP, and SOAP, reducing VOC emissions from industrial sources would have benefits to both the reduction of VOC levels and SOA during pollution episodes. The results here contributed to better-developing countermeasures of alleviating VOCs, ozone, and particulate matter pollutions.", "The limitations of this study were from several aspects: one is the limited known chemistry properties of VOC species; the other is the underestimation of the current modelling; and the third is the assumption of the PMF model. For instance, the ozone reactivity scales for 118 VOC species under different  $\mathrm{NO}_x$  conditions were summarized by Carter (2013). Similarly, Atkinson and Arey (2003) summarized the atmospheric degradation of about 190 VOC species (known as the rate constant  $k_{OH}$ ). A list of the SOAPs relative to toluene for 113 VOC species was also reported by Derwent et al. (2010). Despite the great efforts have been made to study the photochemical characteristics of the VOC species, some VOC species' properties are still unknown. They may be important to the atmospheric reactivity, ozone, and secondary aerosol formation potentials. Limited species (i.e., 33 species in this study) introduced into the PMF model suggested the underestimation of  $\mathrm{L_{OH}}$ , OFP, and SOAP. For SOA modelling, the SOA yield method using chamber study or simulation using volatility basis sets (VBS) underestimated the SOA mass (Chen et al., 2017). evaluation.extracted_insight: 图 6 量化了不同 VOC 来源在四个关键指标上的贡献百分比：总 VOC 混合比 (MR)、OH 损失率 (L_OH)、臭氧生成潜能 (OFP) 与二次有机气溶胶潜能 (SOAP)。例如，工业源对 L_OH 贡献 32.2%，溶剂使用对 L_OH 贡献 21.4%；车辆排放在 OFP 中占 27.8%，工业源占 25.4%。这些数值揭示了各源在不同化学过程中的主导作用。
evaluation.reason: 完整且详细的定量贡献数据覆盖了所有主要化学指标，为制定针对性减排策略提供了强有力的依据。

metadata.content: The figure shows percentage contributions of various VOC sources to VOC mixing ratio, reactivity, ozone formation potential, and secondary organic aerosol potential over different PM2.5 concentration ...

Percentages  \%  of different VOC sources to the total VOC mixing ratio (MR), reactivity  (L_{OH)} , ozone formation potential (OFP), and secondary organic aerosol potential (SOAP) under different  PM_{2.5}  concentration ranges.", "save_path": "C:\liuxingyu\multisubspace-data\predataprocess\paper_md\Source apportionment of volatile organic compounds\figure_7.jpg", "functional_roles": {"main_role": "Results & Findings", "sub_roles": ["Present Quantitative Results", "Compare Experimental Performance"]}, "visual_type": {"main_type": "Quantitative Charts", "sub_types": ["Bar Chart"]}, "key_entities": ["VOC sources", "mixing ratio", "reactivity", "ozone formation potential", "secondary organic aerosol potential", "PM2.5 concentration"], "concise_summary": "The figure shows percentage contributions of various VOC sources to VOC mixing ratio, reactivity, ozone formation potential, and secondary organic aerosol potential over different PM2.5 concentration ranges.", "inferred_insight": "The analysis demonstrates the rising significance of industrial sources of VOCs in conditions of high PM2.5 pollution, highlighting their impact on chemical reactivity and pollution potential.", "references": ["To better understand the differences in levels and reactivity of VOC sources under different air quality, the  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  levels were classified into several groups and the source contributions, OH loss rate, OFP, and SOAP of VOCs were summarized in Fig. 7. Generally, with the increase of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  levels from clean to heavy pollution, the VOC levels also increased from  $40.8 \pm 17.6$  ppbv to  $89.6 \pm 26.3$  ppbv. The same phenomenon was also reported elsewhere (Han et al., 2017; Liu et al., 2017; Hui et al., 2019). Fig. 7 clearly shows that the high  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  pollution was characterized by an increase of industrial sources, which accounted for up to  $27.3\%$ ,  $56.2\%$ ,  $48.4\%$ , and  $57.0\%$  of VOCs mixing ratio, OH loss rate, OFP, and SOAP, respectively. The results here were similar to a previous study that the percentage of industrial sources increased significantly with the increase in VOC levels and this source dominated the VOC origins during an entire severe pollution episode (Hui et al.,", "Volatile organic compounds (VOCs) are important contributors to atmospheric chemical reactivity rate  $(\mathrm{L_{OH}})$ , ozone formation potential (OFP), and secondary organic aerosol formation potential (SOAP). The purpose of this study was to determine the contributions of different VOC sources to  $\mathrm{L_{OH}}$ , OFP, and SOAP using positive matrix factorization (PMF) model. Hourly observation of ambient VOCs at a megacity of Central China was conducted for one month in winter. The  $\mathrm{L_{OH}}$ , OFP, and SOAP of VOC species were firstly estimated. The PMF model was then used to apportion their sources. Five VOC sources including solvent usage, gasoline evaporation, combustion sources, vehicle emissions, and industrial sources were apportioned with their average contributions of  $11.6\%$ ,  $14.2\%$ ,  $39.0\%$ ,  $22.5\%$ , and  $12.6\%$ , respectively. Industrial sources were the highest contributors to the  $\mathrm{L_{OH}}$  and SOAP with average contributions of  $32.2\%$  and  $32.8\%$ , respectively, while vehicle emissions contributed most to the OFP  $(27.8\%)$ . This study highlighted that the VOCs and ozone pollution control should give priority to the reactivity (OFP and SOAP) based strategies rather than concentration contribution-based strategies.", "Fig. 6. Percentages  $(\%)$  of different VOC sources to the total VOC mixing ratio (MR), OH loss rate  $\mathrm{(L_{OH})}$ , ozone formation potential (OFP), and secondary organic aerosol potential (SOAP).", "The limitations of this study were from several aspects: one is the limited known chemistry properties of VOC species; the other is the underestimation of the current modelling; and the third is the assumption of the PMF model. For instance, the ozone reactivity scales for 118 VOC species under different  $\mathrm{NO}_x$  conditions were summarized by Carter (2013). Similarly, Atkinson and Arey (2003) summarized the atmospheric degradation of about 190 VOC species (known as the rate constant  $k_{OH}$ ). A list of the SOAPs relative to toluene for 113 VOC species was also reported by Derwent et al. (2010). Despite the great efforts have been made to study the photochemical characteristics of the VOC species, some VOC species' properties are still unknown. They may be important to the atmospheric reactivity, ozone, and secondary aerosol formation potentials. Limited species (i.e., 33 species in this study) introduced into the PMF model suggested the underestimation of  $\mathrm{L_{OH}}$ , OFP, and SOAP. For SOA modelling, the SOA yield method using chamber study or simulation using volatility basis sets (VBS) underestimated the SOA mass (Chen et al., 2017). metadata.metadata: {"paper_title": "Source apportionment of volatile organic compounds", "figure_id": "img_205", "figure_title": "Fig. 8. Scatter plots between the source profiles derived from the measured and initial VOC levels. The error bars represent the standard deviations of the bootstrap source profiles  N = 100 . The color solid lines represent the linear regression results and the filled ribbons represent the  95\\%  confidence intervals of fitting. The black solid lines represent the 1:1 line.", "save_path": "C:\\liuxingyu\\multisubspace-data\\predataprocess\\paper_md\\Source apportionment of volatile organic compounds\\figure_8.jpg", "functional_roles": {"main_role": "Results & Findings", "sub_roles": ["Present Core Findings", "Compare Experimental Performance"]}, "visual_type": {"main_type": "Quantitative Charts", "sub_types": ["Scatter Plot"]}, "key_entities": ["source profiles", "VOC levels", "PMF model", "gasoline evaporation", "solvent usage", "combustion sources", "industrial sources"], "concise_summary": "The figure displays scatter plots comparing source profiles from measured and initial VOC levels. Linear regressions and confidence intervals are shown, highlighting variability and correlations in source profiles.", "inferred_insight": "The figure illustrates strong correlations for solvent usage and gasoline evaporation, but discrepancies in vehicle emissions suggest variability in photochemical processes impacting VOC profiles.", "references": ["results by the PMF modelling. The measured and initial datasets were both introduced into the PMF model and the source profiles (Fig. 4 and Fig. S3) from two datasets were compared as shown in Fig. 8. The source profiles of gasoline evaporation, solvent usage, combustion sources, and industrial sources derived from two different datasets were in line with each other, with Pearson coefficient  $(r^2)$  of 0.99, 0.85, 0.68, and 0.40, respectively. However, a great discrepancy was found for vehicle emissions. The PMF algorithms aim to find the profiles and contributions for a given number of factors that have the minimum weighed residues of the input dataset. If the importance of atmospheric photochemical processes exceeds the differences of emission profiles from various sources, the factors in PMF can still be identified according to the different degrees of photochemical processing (Yuan et al., 2012). The source profiles derived from the two datasets suggested that the importance of photochemistry in the atmosphere exceeded the differences in the VOC compositions of vehicle emissions. Compared to the source apportionment from the measured dataset, the contributions of solvent usage, gasoline evaporation, and industrial sources to total VOC mixing ratio derived from the initial dataset were higher, while vehicle emissions and combustion sources were lower (Fig. S4). The differences suggested that the VOC decay should be considered before source apportionment using the receptor model."], "chunk_type": "figure"}

args.query_intent: R ggplot2 visualization of air pollution time series and spatial distribution

orchestrator_plan.reason: 现有检索多聚焦于 Python、GIS 和仪表盘，缺少基于 R 语言的可视化方法。通过检索 ggplot2 示例可获取高质量的时间序列曲线和空间热图，为多学科研究提供不同技术栈的参考。
orchestrator_plan.plansummary: 这个策略的目标是弥补当前检索中对 R 语言 ggplot2 在空气污染时间序列和空间分布可视化方面的缺失，期望直接获得基于 ggplot2 的示例代码或图形方案，以便为使用不同技术栈的研究人员提供参考。实际检索得到的十条结果全部围绕空气污染的空间格局和时间变化展开，但均未出现 R 或 ggplot2 的内容；它们主要展示了亚洲或美国地区的排放网格图、NO₂、HCHO 等气体的卫星列值图、以线图呈现的SO₂、CO、PM2.5 等月份变化、基于 ArcGIS/GeoDa 的空间自相关分析以及燃烧率和 VOC 时间序列的箱线图。这些材料共同提供了丰富的污染物空间分布和季节性波动的可视化思路，暗示了可以借鉴的图形类型（热图、线图、箱线图）和数据来源（高分辨率排放清单、卫星观测），但仍缺乏直接的 R / ggplot2 实现细节。

content.text: The figure displays grid maps for gaseous and aerosol species based on the MIX Asian emission inventory in 2010, highlighting spatial emission patterns. The maps illustrate improved spatial emission patterns due to the incorporation of high-resolution datasets, assisting in better understanding and management of emissions across Asia.

{"paper_title": "MIX_ a mosaic Asian anthropogenic emission invento", "figure_id": "img_165", "figure_title": "Figure 12. Grid maps for gaseous (a) and aerosol (b) species in the MIX Asian emission inventory, 2010.", "save_path": "C:\liuxingyu\multisubspace-data\predataprocess\paper_md\MIX_ a mosaic Asian anthropogenic emission invento\figure_12.jpg", "functional_roles": {"main_role": "Results & Findings", "sub_roles": ["Present Core Findings", "Showcase Prediction Results"]}, "visual_type": {"main_type": "Geospatial Visualizations", "sub_types": ["Map"]}, "key_entities": ["MIX Asian emission inventory", "gaseous species", "aerosol species", "2010", "CPED", "JEI-DB", "OPRF"], "concise_summary": "The figure displays grid maps for gaseous and aerosol species based on the MIX Asian emission inventory in 2010, highlighting spatial emission patterns.", "inferred_insight": "The maps illustrate improved spatial emission patterns due to the incorporation of high-resolution datasets, assisting in better understanding and management of emissions across Asia.", "references": ["In the MIX inventory, gridded emissions for 10 gaseous and aerosol species were developed at  $0.25 \times 0.25^{\circ}$  resolution. Emission maps of all species in 2010 are shown in Fig. 12. Compared to the previous gridded Asian emission inventories, we believe the spatial patterns are improved because several local high-resolution emission datasets are incorporated, such as CPED for China and JEI-DB and OPRF for Japan. However, for sectors in which emissions are dominated by spatially scattered sources (e.g., residential combustion, solvent use), the spatial distributions in emissions are still uncertain.", "Figure 12.", "Abstract. The MIX inventory is developed for the years 2008 and 2010 to support the Model Inter-Comparison Study for Asia (MICS-Asia) and the Task Force on Hemispheric Transport of Air Pollution (TF HTAP) by a mosaic of up-to-date regional emission inventories. Emissions are estimated for all major anthropogenic sources in 29 countries and regions in Asia. We conducted detailed comparisons of different regional emission inventories and incorporated the best available ones for each region into the mosaic inventory at a uniform spatial and temporal resolution. Emissions are aggregated to five anthropogenic sectors: power, industry, residential, transportation, and agriculture. We estimate the total Asian emissions of 10 species in 2010 as follows:  $51.3\mathrm{TgSO}_2$ ,  $52.1\mathrm{TgNO}_x$ ,  $336.6\mathrm{TgCO}$ ,  $67.0\mathrm{TgNMVOC}$  (non-methane volatile organic compounds),  $28.8\mathrm{TgNH}_3$", "Generally, there are two common approaches to develop a bottom-up emission inventory at regional level. One is using a unified framework of source categories, calculating method, chemical speciation scheme (if applicable), and spatial and temporal allocations (e.g., Streets et al., 2003; Ohara et al., 2007; Lu et al., 2011). Using the unified approach, emissions are estimated in a consistent way with attainable resources. Several Asian emission inventories widely used in the community were developed by the unified approach. Streets et al. (2003) first developed a comprehensive Asian emission inventory for a variety of gaseous and aerosol species for the year 2000 to support the TRACE-P (Transport and Chemical Evolution over the Pacific) campaign (Carmichael et al., 2003), which was subsequently used for MICS-Asia Phase II. Ohara et al. (2007) developed the Regional Emission inventory in Asia (REAS) version 1.1 covering emissions of major species over Asia from 1980 to 2003, which provides estimates of Asian emissions for a long-term period. However, with the unified approach, many region-dependent parameters are shared among different regions due to lack of resources and local knowledge (e.g., emission factors, chemical profiles, spatial proxies, and temporal profiles, etc.), introducing large uncertainties in emission estimates for a specific region (He et al., 2007; Kurokawa et al., 2009).", "The MIX inventory is developed for 2008 and 2010, in accordance with base year simulations in MICS-Asia III and the Task Force on Hemispheric Transport of Air Pollution (TF HTAP). It should be noted that MIX is not comparable to INTEX-B and TRACE-P to derive an emission trend due to differences in methodology and underlying data. In this paper, we also provided Asian emissions for 2006 using the same methodology, partly resolving the problems of trend analysis in mosaic inventories. The gridded MIX emission data for the years 2008 and 2010 are then incorporated", "Figure 1 presents the definition of the MIX domain and emission datasets used for each country and region. The domain of MIX covers 29 countries and regions (the full list of country and region names are listed in Table 1), stretching from Kazakhstan in the west to Russia Far East in the east and from Indonesia in the south to Siberia in the north. Emissions are aggregated into five sectors: power, industry, residential, transportation, and agriculture. Ten chemical species are included in the MIX inventory, including both gaseous and aerosol species:  $\mathrm{SO}_2$ ,  $\mathrm{NO}_x$ , CO, NMVOC (non-methane volatile organic compounds),  $\mathrm{NH}_3$  (ammonia),  $\mathrm{PM}_{10}$  (particulate matter with diameter less than or equal to  $10\mu \mathrm{m}$ ),  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  (particulate matter with diameter less than or equal to  $2.5\mu \mathrm{m}$ ), BC (black carbon), OC (organic carbon), and  $\mathrm{CO}_2$ . Only emissions from anthropogenic sources are included in MIX. NMVOC emissions are speciated into model-ready inputs for two chemical mechanisms: CB05 (the Carbon Bond mechanism; Yarwood et al., 2005) and SAPRC-99 (the State Air Pollution Research Center 1999 version; Carter, 2000) (see Tables S1 and S2 in the Supplement). Monthly emissions are provided by sector at  $0.25^{\circ} \times 0.25^{\circ}$  resolution. Gridded emissions are available from http://www.meicmodel.org/dataset-mix. The key features of the MIX inventory are summarized in Table 1.", "This paper documents the methodology and emission datasets of the MIX Asian anthropogenic emission inventory. The regional and national inventories used to develop MIX gridded datasets and the mosaic methodology are presented in Sect. 2. Section 3 presents Asian emissions in 2010 and spatial and temporal variations in emissions. Changes in Asian emissions between 2006 and 2010 are also discussed. Section 4 highlights the major improvements in the new inventory by comparing MIX with other Asian emission in", "NMVOC emissions were speciated to SAPRC-99 and CB05 mechanisms following the explicit species mapping approach documented in Li et al. (2014) (see Fig. 3). Finally, emissions were aggregated to the five MIX sectors and then assembled to monthly emission grid maps over Asia with a uniform spatial resolution of  $0.25^{\circ} \times 0.25^{\circ}$ .", "Based on the mosaic approach and candidate inventories described in Sect. 2, gridded anthropogenic emissions for 10 species were generated over Asia and called the MIX emission inventory. In the MIX inventory, Asian anthropogenic emissions in 2010 are estimated as follows:  $51.3\mathrm{TgSO}_2$ $52.1\mathrm{TgNO}_x$ $336.6\mathrm{TgCO}$ $67.0\mathrm{TgNMVOC}$ $28.8\mathrm{TgNH_3}$ $31.7\mathrm{TgPM}_{10}$ $22.7\mathrm{TgPM}_{2.5}$ $3.5\mathrm{TgBC}$ $8.3\mathrm{TgOC}$ , and  $17.3\mathrm{PgCO}_2$ . Figure 4 presents the emission distributions among sectors over Asia in 2010. Among the different sectors, the industrial sector has the largest contribution to  $\mathrm{SO}_2$  (50% of total), NMVOC (38%),  $\mathrm{PM}_{10}$  (48%), and  $\mathrm{CO}_2$  (40%) emissions. Power plants have significant contributions for  $\mathrm{SO}_2$  (38% of total),  $\mathrm{NO}_x$  (29%), and  $\mathrm{CO}_2$  (34%) emissions.", "Table 3. National anthropogenic emissions in the MIX emission inventory in 2010 (units: Tg for  $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$  and Gg for other species). Bold values are the total emissions for Asian regions.", "Ratios of different species were widely used to inform emission characteristics. For example,  $\mathrm{SO}_2 / \mathrm{CO}_2$  ratio was used as an indicator of coal combustion and emission control levels (Li et al., 2007), and ratios of  $\mathrm{CO} / \mathrm{CO}_2$  were used to inform combustion efficiency (Wang et al., 2010). Figure 7 compares regional emission ratios of  $\mathrm{SO}_2 / \mathrm{CO}_2$  and  $\mathrm{CO} / \mathrm{CO}_2$  estimated by the MIX inventory. Emission ratios of  $\mathrm{SO}_2 / \mathrm{CO}_2$  are lowest in Other East Asia among different regions, which could be attributed to small share of coal use", "In this work, we also developed Asian emissions for 2006 and 2008 following the same approach of MIX, to illustrate the changes in Asian emissions from 2006 to 2010. Table 5 presents Asian emissions in 2006 and emission ratios of 2010 to 2016 by country. For the whole of Asia, emission growth rates from 2006 to 2010 are estimated as follows:  $-8.1\%$  for  $\mathrm{SO}_2$ ,  $+19.2\%$  for  $\mathrm{NO}_x$ ,  $+3.9\%$  for  $\mathrm{CO}$ ,  $+15.5\%$  for NMVOC,  $+1.7\%$  for  $\mathrm{NH}_3$ ,  $-3.4\%$  for  $\mathrm{PM}_{10}$ ,  $-1.6\%$  for  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ ,  $+5.5\%$  for BC,  $+1.8\%$  for OC, and  $+19.9\%$  for  $\mathrm{CO}_2$ . Growth in  $\mathrm{CO}_2$  emissions represent the continuously increasing energy use across Asia during 2006-2010, while different trends among species represents differences in the emission control level among sectors and regions. Compared to the increasing emission trends of all species during 2001-2006 (Zhang et al., 2009), the relatively flat or even decreasing emission trends in many species indicate the effectiveness of emission control measures in recent years (Gu et al., 2013; Lin et al., 2010; Wang et al., 2013).", "MIX emission inventory can be accessed publicly from the website of http://www.meicmodel.org/dataset-mix. Both 2008 and 2010 emissions of 10 species with monthly variation at a spatial resolution of  $0.25 \times 0.25^{\circ}$  are available from the website, including  $\mathrm{SO}_2$ ,  $\mathrm{NO}_x$ , CO,  $\mathrm{NH}_3$ , NMVOC,  $\mathrm{PM}_{10}$ ,  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ , BC, OC, and  $\mathrm{CO}_2$ . Speciated NMVOC Emissions for CB05 and SAPRC-99 chemical mechanisms are provided at the same spatial and temporal resolution. The MIX in", "with REAS2 and EDGAR v4.2 (EC-JRC/PBL, 2011), two widely used inventories, to highlight the new findings from the mosaic inventory and identify the potential sources of uncertainties. We choose the year of 2008 to conduct the comparison because emissions after 2008 are not available in either REAS2 or EDGAR v4.2. Russian Asia was excluded from comparison. Asian anthropogenic emissions of MIX, REAS2, and EDGAR v4.2 in 2008 are tabulated in Table 7. Over Asia, MIX and REAS differ within  $10\%$  for most species, except for  $\mathrm{NH}_3$  ( $18\%$  higher in REAS),  $\mathrm{PM}_{10}$  ( $13\%$  higher), and BC ( $13\%$  lower). It is not surprising that the total Asian emission budgets in MIX and REAS2 are simi", "The MIX emission inventory subjects to uncertainties and several limitations. Emission estimates from bottom-up inventories are uncertain due to lack of complete knowledge of human activities and emission from different sources. Uncertainty ranges of an emission inventory could be estimated using propagation of error or Monte Carlo approaches (e.g., Streets et al., 2003; Zhao et al., 2011). However, in a mosaic emission inventory like MIX, a normalized quantitative assessment of uncertainty ranges is difficult because detailed information for emission inventory development is not collected. Table 9 summarized the uncertainty range estimates for China, India, and other Asian regions in different regional emission inventories. It should be noted that those ranges are not directly comparable due to differences in methods (propagation of error or Monte Carlo simulation). However, those numbers might roughly represent the uncertainty ranges in the MIX inventory as it was compiled from several inventories listed in Table 9. In general, uncertainty ranges are relatively small for species which emissions are dominated my large-scale combustion sources (e.g.,  $\mathrm{SO}_2$ ,  $\mathrm{NO}_x$ , and  $\mathrm{CO}_2$ ) but larger for species whose emissions are mainly from small-scale and scattered sources (e.g., CO, NMVOC, and carbonaceous aerosols). More detailed discussions on the uncertainty sources of Asian emission inventories can be found in previous literatures (e.g., Lu et al., 2011; Zhao et al., 2011; Kurokawa et al., 2013).", "When species in a specific inventory were missing, alternative estimates or datasets were used to fill the gap in which may involve additional uncertainties. In the MIX inventory,  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ , BC, and OC emissions for Republic of Korea were roughly estimates from  $\mathrm{PM}_{10}$  emissions in the CAPSS inventory and sector-specific emission ratios between  $\mathrm{PM}_{10}$  and other aerosol components from Lei et al. (2011). For India, we used ANL-India for  $\mathrm{SO}_2$ , BC, and OC for all sectors and  $\mathrm{NO}_x$  for power plants. REAS2 was used to fill the gap where emissions from ANL-India were absent (see Sect. 2.3 for detailed process procedure). For above cases where estimates of different species in the same country were obtained from", "In this work, we developed a new anthropogenic emission inventory for Asia for the years of 2008 and 2010 by constructing a mosaic of several regional and national emission inventories. MEIC, PKU-NH $_3$ , ANL-India, and CAPSS inventories are used to represent the best available emission data for China, India, and Korea, supplemented with REAS2 to fill gaps. By harmonizing these inventories, monthly emission grids maps for 10 species over Asia were generated for five sectors (power, industry, residential, transportation, and agriculture) at a uniform spatial resolution of  $0.25^{\circ} \times 0.25^{\circ}$ . Gridded speciated NMVOC emissions for SAPRC-99 and CB05 mechanisms were also developed at the same temporal and spatial resolution. This new Asian emission inventory, named MIX, provides model-ready anthropogenic emissions for the MICS-Asia Phase III assessment. The MIX inventory has been also incorporated into the HTAP v2 gridded emission inventory (Janssens-Maenhout et al., 2015) to support the TF HTAP assessment. Gridded emissions are available from the following website: http://www.meicmodel.org/dataset-mix.", "Guan, D., Liu, Z., Geng, Y., Lindner, S., and Hubacek, K.: The gigatonne gap in China's carbon dioxide inventories, Nature Clim. Change, 2, 672-675, 2012.

content.text: Figure 11 illustrates monthly variations in SO2, CO, PM2.5, and CO2 emissions across Asian regions for 2010 using line charts. The figure highlights regional differences in monthly emission patterns, revealing distinct seasonal peaks and variations in the emissions of key pollutants across Asia.

Monthly variations of Asian $\mathrm{SO}_2$ , CO, $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ , and $\mathrm{CO}_2$ emissions by sector for the year 2010. ![](https://cdn-mineru.openxlab.org.cn/result/2025-12-03/fa4e3fc1-fa8a-45bf-892c-02fa70317f3a/9f2ea3261ee32bb0c63e3b3f77bf598158a7f14fca17a9e7de52657445eb7c5a.jpg) SO2 CO PM25 CO2", "Monthly emissions by sector and by Asian region are provided in Tables S6-S14. Monthly profiles in emissions are highly sector dependent given that monthly activity rates vary among different sectors. Figure 10 illustrates the monthly variations of Asian $\mathrm{SO}_2$ , CO, $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ , and $\mathrm{CO}_2$ emissions by sector for the year 2010. Different species generally show similar monthly emission patterns within the same sector, indicating that monthly emission profiles of each sector are dominated by monthly variations in activity rates. For example, industrial emissions are higher in the second half of the year induced by larger industrial productions to meet the annual total production target. The most significant monthly variation with a winter peak was found in the residential sector, reflecting the higher energy demand for residential heating in winter. Residential $\mathrm{SO}_2$ emissions in winter are even higher than other species, because $\mathrm{SO}_2$ emissions from China dominate residential emissions in Asia (70% of total), of which coal consumption in winter is higher than other regions for heating. Monthly profiles of CO emissions are different from other species for the transportation sector. This is because the CO emission factor in winter is higher than in other seasons due to additional emissions from the cold-start process (Kurokawa et al., 2013; Zheng et al., 2014)."], "chunk_type": "figure"}

content.text: The figure shows the spatial distribution of OMI tropospheric NO2 and HCHO vertical column densities across CONUS for the summers of 2011, 2014, and 2017. The figure illustrates decreases in NO2 columns over time, highlighting patterns in air quality and pollution reduction across urban and rural regions, emphasizing the importance of monitoring and implementing emission control policies.

metadata.metadata: {"paper_title": "Changes in the ozone chemical regime over the cont", "figure_id": "img_43", "figure_title": "Fig. 2. Spatial distribution of (a to c) the OMI tropospheric  NO_2  vertical column density and (d to f) the OMI HCHO vertical column density in the summers (June-August) of 2011, 2014, and 2017.", "save_path": "C:\\liuxingyu\\multisubspace-data\\predataprocess\\paper_md\\Changes in the ozone chemical regime over the cont\\figure_2.jpg", "functional_roles": {"main_role": "Results & Findings", "sub_roles": ["Present Core Findings", "Present Quantitative Results"]}, "visual_type": {"main_type": "Geospatial Visualizations", "sub_types": ["Map"]}, "key_entities": ["OMI", "tropospheric NO2", "HCHO", "vertical column density", "CONUS"], "concise_summary": "The figure shows the spatial distribution of OMI tropospheric NO2 and HCHO vertical column densities across CONUS for the summers of 2011, 2014, and 2017.", "inferred_insight": "The figure illustrates decreases in NO2 columns over time, highlighting patterns in air quality and pollution reduction across urban and rural regions, emphasizing the importance of monitoring and implementing emission control policies.", "references": ["Fig. 2 illustrates the spatial distribution of seasonally averaged tropospheric  $\\mathrm{NO}_2$  and total HCHO vertical column densities from the OMI for the summers (June to August) of 2011, 2014, and 2017 over the CONUS. The AMFs of both columns were adjusted based on the  $\\mathrm{NO}_2$  and HCHO columns simulated in this study. The OMI tropospheric  $\\mathrm{NO}_2$  columns showed large values in cities with high population density and industrial activity, such as Seattle, Los Angeles, Houston, Chicago, and New York. During the study period, the  $\\mathrm{NO}_2$  columns exhibited a similar spatial distribution, but with a domain-wide decrease by  $-6.10\\%$  and  $-7.95\\%$  in 2014 and 2017, respectively, with respect to 2011. With regard to HCHO columns, high values were predominant in the southeastern U. S., mainly resulting from oxidation of biogenically emitted isoprene", "Figs. 5 and 6 display the spatial distribution of averaged tropospheric  $\\mathrm{NO}_2$  and total HCHO column densities simulated with prior and posterior emissions corresponding to the OMI observations (shown in Fig. 2) and their differences with respect to the column with prior emissions. For both the  $\\mathrm{NO}_2$  and HCHO columns, the modeled columns with posterior emissions more closely agreed with those from the OMI observations. In most areas, the modeled tropospheric  $\\mathrm{NO}_2$  column, compared to that of the OMI observations, exhibited an underestimation throughout the study period (Figs. 2 and 5), but using posterior emission (shown in Fig. S5), we observed a large reduction in bias, increasing the  $\\mathrm{NO}_2$  column by as much as  $21.55 \\pm 28.76\\%$  nationwide. The adjustment ratio between the prior and posterior  $\\mathrm{NO}_x$  emissions was slightly high in 2017  $(2.84 \\pm 1.99)$  than in 2011  $(2.33 \\pm 1.83)$  and 2014  $(2.62 \\pm 2.19)$  over the CONUS (Fig. S8), predominantly in the central and western", "To investigate changes in the ozone  $(\\mathrm{O}_3)$  chemical production regime over the contiguous United States (CONUS) with accurate knowledge of concentrations of its precursors, we applied an inverse modeling technique with Ozone Monitoring Instrument (OMI) tropospheric nitrogen dioxide  $(\\mathrm{NO}_2)$  and total formaldehyde (HCHO) retrieval products in the summers of 2011, 2014, and 2017, years in which United States National Emission Inventory were based. The inclusion of dynamic chemical lateral boundary conditions and lightning-induced nitric oxide emissions significantly account for the contribution of background sources in the free troposphere. Satellite-constrained nitrogen oxide  $(\\mathrm{NO}_{\\mathrm{x}})$  and non-methane volatile organic compounds (NMVOCs) emissions mitigate the discrepancy between satellite and modeled columns: the inversion suggested 2.33-2.84 (1.07-1.34) times higher  $\\mathrm{NO}_{\\mathrm{x}}$  over the CONUS (over urban regions) and 0.28-0.81 times fewer NMVOCs emissions over the southeastern United States. The model-derived HCHO/NO $_2$  column ratio shows gradual spatial changes in the  $\\mathrm{O}_3$  production regime near urban cores relative to previously defined threshold values representing  $\\mathrm{NO}_{\\mathrm{x}}$  and VOC sensitive conditions. We also found apparent shifts from the  $\\mathrm{NO}_{\\mathrm{x}}$ -saturated regime to the transition regime (or the transition regime to the  $\\mathrm{NO}_{\\mathrm{x}}$ -limited regime) over the major cities in the western United States. In contrast, rural areas, especially in the east-southeastern United States, exhibit a decreased HCHO/NO $_2$  column ratio by  $-1.30 \\pm 1.71$  with a reduction in HCHO column primarily driven by meteorology, becoming sensitive to VOC emissions. Results show that incorporating satellite observations into numerical modeling could help policymakers implement appropriate emission control policies for  $\\mathrm{O}_3$  pollution.", "Fig. 5. Spatial distribution of the modeled  $\\mathrm{NO}_2$  vertical column densities with prior and posterior emissions and their relative differences with respect to the modeled column density with prior emissions in the summers (June-August) of 2011, 2014, and 2017.", "Fig. 6. Spatial distribution of the modeled HCHO vertical column densities with prior and posterior emissions, and their relative differences with respect to the modeled column density with prior emissions in the summers (June-August) 2011, 2014, and 2017.", "Fig. 8 (a) shows the spatial distribution of the ozone chemical production regime (based on the averaged HCHO/NO $_2$  ratio) over the CONUS in the summer of 2011, while the results for 2014 and 2017 are plotted in Fig. S11. The entire CONUS is divided into three regimes according to the threshold values we defined earlier; model grids with NO $_2$  column density less than  $1 \\times 10^{15}$  molecules  $\\mathrm{cm}^{-2}$  are not considered and are denoted by the gray color. Several metropolitan cities were categorized as  $\\mathrm{NO}_x$ -saturated and transition regimes, whereas the remaining rural areas were classified as  $\\mathrm{NO}_x$ -limited regimes due to the nature of urban conditions with highly concentrated traffic density and amount of vehicle emissions. According to Fig. 8(b), showing the difference of HCHO/NO $_2$  ratio between 2011 and 2017, the column ratio in rural areas decreased by  $-1.30 \\pm 1.71$ , mostly over the eastern and southeastern U.S. This regime modification toward the  $\\mathrm{NO}_x$ -saturated condition could be explained by the increased  $\\mathrm{NO}_x$  emissions over the northeastern U.S. (Fig. S7), as well as the sustained decrease of the HCHO column (Fig. 3 (h-2)) as a response to meteorological change (Fig. S2).", "(b) Difference of HCHO/NO  ${}_{2}$  ratio between 2011 and 2017  \nFig. 8. (a) Spatial distribution of the ozone chemical production regime over the CONUS for the year 2011. (b) The difference between the  $\\mathrm{HCHO / NO_2}$  ratios of 2011 and 2017. Gray areas represent tropospheric  $\\mathrm{NO}_2$  column densities that are less than  $1\\times 10^{15}$  molecules  $\\mathrm{cm}^{-2}$ .", "In this study, we examined changes in the ozone chemical regime over the CONUS in the summers of 2011, 2014, and 2017, years in which U.S. NEI data were based. To combine the benefits of both satellite retrievals and CTMs, we employed an inverse modeling technique with OMI tropospheric  $\\mathrm{NO}_2$  and total HCHO column retrievals to estimate  $\\mathrm{NO}_{\\mathrm{x}}$  and NMVOCs emissions. To avoid misalignment of the emission adjustment, we applied dynamic chemical lateral boundary conditions and lightning-induced nitric oxide emissions, resulting in better representation of  $\\mathrm{NO}_2$  and  $\\mathrm{O}_3$  concentration in the upper atmosphere, confirmed by comparisons with measurements from the DISCOVER-AQ campaigns and two ozonesonde sites."], "chunk_type": "figure"}

content.text: To further analyze the temporal and spatial variation features of air quality in 31 provincial capital cities from 2013 to 2020, the spatial autocorrelation analysis method is applied. ArcGIS 10.6 and GeoDa software is used for the calculation and analytic process.

content.text: The harmonization and improvement of regional emission inventories is imperative to obtain consolidated estimates on the formation of global-scale air pollution. An emissions data set has been constructed using regional emission grid maps (annual and monthly) for  $\mathrm{SO}_2$ ,  $\mathrm{NO}_x$ , CO, NMVOC,  $\mathrm{NH}_3$ ,  $\mathrm{PM}_{10}$ ,  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ , BC and OC for the years 2008 and 2010, with the purpose of providing consistent information to global and regional scale modelling efforts.

content.text: After the spatial gatherings of air pollution are confirmed, to better identify the gathering district, a regional spatial autocorrelation analysis is also conducted. As shown in Fig. 5, there were still high-high gathering areas (high levels of pollution) and low-low gathering areas (low levels of pollution) from 2013 to 2020. Meanwhile, a north-south spatial heterogeneity pattern was obvious, depicting the high levels of pollution in northern China and low levels of pollution in southern China.

content.text: The figure illustrates time series, boxplots, and diurnal variations of VOC groups during the observational period, indicating higher pollution levels in Wuhan. The study suggests serious VOC pollution in Wuhan, higher than other regions, emphasizing the environmental challenge and need for targeted air quality policies.

args.query_intent: interactive AQI map visualization using Google Earth Engine

orchestrator_plan.reason: 已有的搜索覆盖了 GIS、Sankey、3D 等可视化，但未涉及利用 Google Earth Engine 直接生成空气质量指数（AQI）地图的案例。该方向可提供全球尺度、动态更新的 AQI 可视化方案，补充现有图形化手段。
orchestrator_plan.plansummary: 该策略旨在弥补现有检索中缺少“利用 Google Earth Engine（GEE）直接生成交互式 AQI 地图”的案例，希望通过 GEE 提供全球尺度、实时更新的空气质量可视化方案。但实际检索得到的十条文献全部聚焦于传统的 AQI 统计图与空间分析——包括 AQI 变化率（如 2013‑2020 年的趋势图）、区域性 AQI 柱状图、空间自相关图、以及基于不同排放清单绘制的网格地图或模拟域示意图。这些结果集中展示了中国大陆或美国部分地区的 AQI 时空分布、政策影响和排放特征，却没有任何关于 GEE 平台、交互式地图或动态可视化的实现细节。因此，虽然检索到的内容在空气质量空间分布和趋势分析方面提供了丰富的背景信息，但并未满足策略设定的核心需求——即寻找使用 Google Earth Engine 进行 AQI 可视化的具体案例或技术路径。

The inputs for MEGAN include the LAI, PFTs, emission factors, and meteorological data (e.g., solar radiation, temperature, relative humidity and soil moisture). The monthly average LAI was obtained from the 8-day MODIS LAI product (MOD15A2, 2017) with the same horizontal resolution as the study domain, as shown in Fig. 2. The PFT map was obtained from the MODIS MCD12Q1 product and regridded into the WRF domain to compute the fraction of each PFT in each grid (Fig. 3). The default MEGAN emission factors  $(\mathrm{mg~m}^{-2}\mathrm{h}^{-1})$  with a resolution of  $30\mathrm{s}$ $(< 1\mathrm{km})$ , which reflect the BVOC emission rate under standard canopy conditions, were leveraged (http://lar.wsu.edu/megan/guides.html). Meteorological conditions were simulated by the WRF model. A one-year MEGAN simulation was performed for 2017 in China.", "We set up Community Multiscale Air Quality model (CMAQ version 5.2.1, Foroutan et al. (2017)) (https://www.cmascenter.org/cmaq/) to simulate the atmospheric composition over China with the same domain and grid resolution for WRF and MEGAN. The initial and boundary conditions for CMAQ simulation were based on the CMAQ default profiles which represent unpolluted atmosphere. The CB05 represented the gas phase, and AERO6 represented the aerosol chemical mechanisms (Appel et al. 2013). For the simulation of SOA, we used the default SOA module of the CMAQv5.2.1. It simulates NMVOC-derived SOA with a two-product model, treats primary organic aerosol (POA) as nonvolatile and nonreactive, and ignores IVOC emissions. Carlton et al. (2010) provided a detailed description of aerosol chemistry in CMAQ, including SOA formation from benzene, isoprene and sesquiterpenes. The POA oxidation mechanism is described in Simon and Bhave (2012). The new updates in CMAQ v5.2.1 accounts for the semivolatile", "partitioning and gas-phase aging of these POA compounds consistent with experimentally derived parameterizations (Murphy et al. 2017), SOA properties and IEPOX organosulfate formation rate constant were updated (Pye et al. 2017). We selected July as representative of summertime and acquired anthropogenic emissions for the CMAQ domain from the MEIC emission inventory 2016 developed by Tsinghua University (http://www.meicmodel.org/), which contains monthly gridded  $(0.25\times 0.25^{\circ})$  emissions information for anthropogenic emissions in the CB05 mechanism. The biogenic emissions were modeled using MEGAN (described in Section 2.2).", "Fig. S1 and S2 show the seasonal spatial distribution of temperature at  $2\mathrm{m}$  (T2) and the daily downward shortwave radiation (DSW) simulated by the WRF model. And these meteorological factors were evaluated using in situ measurement data from 824 and 84 national meteorological sites, respectively. The in situ measurement data are from the China Meteorological Data Sharing Service System (http://data.cma.cn/). Table 2 presents the verification statistics for the average daily T2 and DSW among all sites. As shown in Table 2, the mean error (ME), mean bias (MB), correlation coefficient (r) and root mean square error (RMSE) of station-averaged hourly T2 series are 2.47, -1.26, 0.95 and  $2.59^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , respectively. The simulation yields cooling biases of  $-0.98$ ,  $-0.90$ ,  $-1.70$  and  $-1.18^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$  in spring, summer, fall and winter, respectively. The ME, MB, r and RMSE values for the annual DSW series are 36.00,  $-29.88$ , 0.80 and  $81.33\mathrm{Wm}^{-2}$ , and the DSW simulation yields underestimations of 32.12, 43.61, 24.25 and  $19.38\mathrm{Wm}^{-2}$  in spring, summer, fall and winter, respectively. It is because that the WRF model overestimate the cloud coverage which lead to the slightly underestimation on T2 and DSW (Wang et al. 2010; Wen et al. 2014).
